title: Ezekiel 22
subtitle: The Bloody City

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1-16 — A Catalogue of Jerusalem’s Abominations

heb.paragraph: The plural 'bloods' (damim) intensifies the singular — this is not a single act of violence but systemic, pervasive bloodshed. Jerusalem has become a city defined by its accumulated blood-guilt. The word echoes Cain’s murder (Gen 4:10) where Abel’s blood 'cries from the ground.'

Ezekiel’s characteristic term of contempt for idols, possibly derived from gelal ('dung'). He uses it 39 times — more than all other biblical authors combined. The word is deliberately crude: idols are excrement, not gods.

refs.note: The sexual prohibitions of the Holiness Code are systematically violated in Jerusalem — Ezekiel 22 reverses Leviticus 18 point by point.
Isaiah’s earlier indictment of Jerusalem as a 'faithful city become a prostitute' uses similar categories of social injustice.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem as the city that 'kills the prophets' — the blood-guilt tradition continues to the NT.

notes.note: The phrase 'city of bloodshed' is a title of condemnation. The same phrase is used of Nineveh in Nahum 3:1 — Jerusalem has become as wicked as the Assyrian capital.
This catalogue of sins mirrors the Ten Commandments in reverse: dishonour of parents (v.7), Sabbath-breaking (v.8), slander (v.9), sexual immorality (vv.10-11), murder for hire and usury (v.12).
God’s rhetorical question 'Can your courage endure?' implies the answer is no. When God acts, all human bravado collapses.
Dispersion among the nations will purge the people’s impurity. Exile is not just punishment but cleansing — a painful but purposeful act.
Calvin reads the 'bloody city' as a warning to all cities that tolerate injustice. Civic corruption is not merely social failure but covenant treason against God.
Calvin emphasises usury and extortion as sins that reveal the fundamental orientation of the heart — greed replaces trust in God’s provision.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew construction uses the interrogative particle with emphatic force: 'Will you judge? Will you judge the city of bloodshed?' The repetition conveys urgency.
The phrase 'uncover their fathers’ nakedness' is a technical term for sexual violation within the family (cf. Lev 18:7-8).

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies the structure as a three-part indictment: the charge (vv.1-5), the evidence catalogue (vv.6-12), and the sentence (vv.13-16). Each section opens with a divine speech formula. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli notes that this passage gathers together sins scattered across the Holiness Code into a single devastating summary. The effect is cumulative. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: Chapter 22 is structured as a covenant lawsuit (rib) against Jerusalem. God acts as prosecutor, cataloguing sins in three categories: bloodshed, idolatry, and social injustice. The list in vv.6-12 reads like a negative mirror of the Holiness Code (Lev 17-26) — every commandment violated, every social boundary transgressed. Fathers are dishonoured, aliens are oppressed, orphans and widows are mistreated, the Sabbath is profaned, sexual sins abound, and bribery pervades the courts.

heb.paragraph: The smelting metaphor is devastating: Israel has become dross — the worthless residue left after precious metal is refined. In Isaiah 1:22, Israel’s silver has become dross; here in Ezekiel, the people themselves are the dross. There is no precious metal left to extract.

refs.note: Egypt was the iron-smelting furnace of redemption; Jerusalem becomes the furnace of judgment — a devastating reversal of the exodus.
Isaiah uses the same smelting metaphor but with hope: God will smelt away the dross and restore the pure metal.
Malachi prophesies a future refining where the Levites will be purified like gold and silver.

notes.note: Israel has become 'dross' — all of them bronze, tin, iron, and lead. These are the base metals that remain after silver is refined.
God gathers the people into Jerusalem 'to melt you' — the siege is divine action, not merely Babylonian military strategy.
The recognition formula concludes: 'Then you will know that I the LORD have poured out my wrath upon you.'

Calvin draws the pastoral lesson: God tests his people as a refiner tests metal. Those who prove to be dross will be consumed by the process. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The list of base metals (bronze, tin, iron, lead) represents the full range of impurities. The text emphasises 'all of them.'. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block notes that the smelting metaphor was common in ancient Near Eastern judgment imagery. Ezekiel’s innovation is the totality of the condemnation. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies this as a 'proof-saying' (Erweiswort) structured around the recognition formula. The furnace is annihilation, not purification. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: The smelting furnace metaphor reverses the exodus tradition. In Deuteronomy 4:20, Egypt was the 'iron-smelting furnace' from which God rescued Israel. Now Jerusalem itself becomes the furnace — but this time there is no rescue. The people are gathered into the city not for protection but for destruction.

sections.header: Verses 23-31 — No One to Stand in the Gap

heb.paragraph: The 'gap' (perets) in the wall is both literal and metaphorical. A city wall with a breach is defenceless; a society without intercessors is spiritually defenceless. God searched for someone to 'stand in the gap' (v.30) but found no one.

refs.note: Moses 'stood in the breach' before God to prevent destruction — the intercessory role that no one fills in Ezekiel’s Jerusalem.
God 'was appalled that there was no one to intervene' — so his own arm worked salvation.
Paul identifies Jesus as the one mediator between God and humanity — the ultimate answer to the 'gap.'

notes.note: The 'conspiracy of prophets' describes false prophets who collude to deceive the people for profit. They are compared to roaring lions tearing prey.
The priests 'do violence to my law' by failing to distinguish between holy and common, clean and unclean.
False prophets 'whitewash' the wall with plaster — the same metaphor from chapter 13.
The search for one righteous intercessor recalls Abraham’s intercession for Sodom (Gen 18:23-32). Here God finds no one at all.

Calvin sees the dignity and necessity of intercessory prayer. God searches for someone to 'stand in the gap' — a single faithful person could have averted national judgment. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Some Hebrew manuscripts and the LXX read 'princes' (nesi’eha) instead of 'prophets' (nevi’eha). The NET Bible follows the LXX reading.
The phrase 'stand before me in the gap' uses military language — the intercessor is a spiritual warrior defending the breach.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block structures this oracle as a 'leadership audit': five groups are indicted in descending social order. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies this as one of the most theologically significant verses in Ezekiel: God actively seeks an intercessor but finds none. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: The final oracle indicts every class of Israelite leadership: prophets (v.25), priests (v.26), officials (v.27), false prophets (v.28), and the people of the land (v.29). Each group has failed its covenant responsibility. God searched for an intercessor and found none (v.30).

themes.note: Judgment and justice dominate as God catalogues Jerusalem’s systematic violation of every covenant obligation.

rows.label: vv.23-31
rows.text: Leadership Audit: No One in the Gap

lit.note: Three oracles progress from indictment through metaphor to comprehensive leadership failure.

hebtext.note: The plural 'bloods' (damim) intensifies the singular — this is not a single act of violence but systemic, pervasive bloodshed. Jerusalem has become a city defined by its accumulated blood-guilt. The word echoes Cain’s murder (Gen 4:10) where Abel’s blood 'cries from the ground.'

Ezekiel’s characteristic term of contempt for idols, possibly derived from gelal ('dung'). He uses it 39 times — more than all other biblical authors combined. The word is deliberately crude: idols are excrement, not gods.

The smelting metaphor is devastating: Israel has become dross — the worthless residue left after precious metal is refined. In Isaiah 1:22, Israel’s silver has become dross; here in Ezekiel, the people themselves are the dross. There is no precious metal left to extract.

The 'gap' (perets) in the wall is both literal and metaphorical. A city wall with a breach is defenceless; a society without intercessors is spiritually defenceless. God searched for someone to 'stand in the gap' (v.30) but found no one.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel is a key figure in The Bloody City.

ppl.name: Daniel
ppl.text: Daniel is not mentioned in Ezekiel 22 and is not a key figure in this chapter.

ppl.name: Noah
ppl.text: Noah is not mentioned in Ezekiel 22 and is not a key figure in this chapter.

ppl.name: Job
ppl.text: Job is not mentioned in Ezekiel 22 and is not a key figure in this chapter.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in The Bloody City.

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Bloody City

translations.text: “ ‘See how each of the princes of Israel who are in you uses his power to shed blood.
“Behold, the princes of Israel in you, every one according to his power, have been bent on shedding blood.

you have become guilty because of the blood you have shed and have become defiled by the idols you have made. You have brought your days to a close, and the end of your years has come. Therefore I will make you an object of scorn to the nations and a laughingstock to all the countries.

You have become guilty by the blood that you have shed, and defiled by the idols that you have made, and you have brought your days near, the appointed time of your years has come. Therefore I have made you a reproach to the nations, and a mockery to all the countries.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The phrase 'city of bloodshed' is a title of condemnation. The same phrase is used of Nineveh in Nahum 3:1 — Jerusalem has become as wicked as the Assyrian capital."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin reads the 'bloody city' as a warning to all cities that tolerate injustice. Civic corruption is not merely social failure but covenant treason against God."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The Hebrew construction uses the interrogative particle with emphatic force: 'Will you judge? Will you judge the city of bloodshed?' The repetition conveys urgency."

thread.text: The sexual prohibitions of the Holiness Code are systematically violated in Jerusalem — Ezekiel 22 reverses Leviticus 18 point by point.
Isaiah’s earlier indictment of Jerusalem as a 'faithful city become a prostitute' uses similar categories of social injustice.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem as the city that 'kills the prophets' — the blood-guilt tradition continues to the NT.
Egypt was the iron-smelting furnace of redemption; Jerusalem becomes the furnace of judgment — a devastating reversal of the exodus.
Isaiah uses the same smelting metaphor but with hope: God will smelt away the dross and restore the pure metal.
Malachi prophesies a future refining where the Levites will be purified like gold and silver.

coaching.tip: The catalogue of sins (vv. 6–12) moves from public violence to private exploitation to sexual immorality to economic corruption. Every sector of society is indicted: princes, prophets, priests, landowners. Ezekiel's diagnosis is systemic — not one bad actor but institutional rot. When he asks 'Will you judge this city of blood?' (v. 2), the answer is: nobody is left to judge because the judges are complicit.

'I looked for someone among them who would build up the wall and stand before me in the gap on behalf of the land so I would not have to destroy it, but I found no one' (v. 30). God searched for an intercessor and found none. The verse implies God wanted to be stopped — wanted someone to plead effectively. Abraham bargained for Sodom; Moses stood in the gap at Sinai. This time, no one showed up. Judgment falls not because God is eager but because intercession failed.

prayer_prompt: As God searches for someone to stand in the gap and finds no one, consider asking God whether He is looking for you to stand between judgment and someone who cannot stand on their own.
